pathophysiology_summary: Viral conjunctivitis (usually adenovirus) is a self-limiting, highly contagious conjunctival infection, often following or accompanying a URI, presenting with a red, gritty eye, watery discharge, morning crusting, a follicular tarsal reaction and a tender preauricular lymph node, frequently starting in one eye then spreading; vision is preserved (any blur clears when the discharge is wiped). It resolves over 1–3 weeks and antibiotics are not indicated. The ground-truth task is to distinguish it from bacterial conjunctivitis (thick purulent discharge, matting) and allergic conjunctivitis (itch-predominant, bilateral, atopy), and — critically — to exclude sight-threatening red-eye causes: keratitis (including HSV), anterior uveitis and acute angle-closure glaucoma, whose danger signs are reduced vision (not just blur from tears), severe deep eye pain, photophobia, halos and ciliary flush (contact-lens wearers are higher risk). Management is supportive (lubricants, cool compresses, hygiene) with strict contagion control.

differential.evidence_basis: Red gritty eye with watery discharge, morning crusting, post-URI, spreading eye-to-eye.
Screen reduced vision, severe deep pain, photophobia, halos, ciliary flush (esp. contact-lens use).
Thick purulent matting discharge — argues against (this is watery).
Itch-predominant with atopy — grittiness over itch and a viral prodrome favour viral.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Viral conjunctivitis leads; sight-threatening red-eye causes must be actively excluded before reassurance.

differential.evidence_basis: Preserved vision, no severe pain/photophobia/halos, watery discharge, follicular reaction, preauricular node, post-URI — viral conjunctivitis.
No red-eye danger signs; escalation target if they develop.
Watery (not purulent) discharge with a preauricular node favours viral.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative red-eye-danger-sign screen, viral conjunctivitis leads; supportive care + strict contagion control, no antibiotics.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; self-limiting over 1–3 weeks; highly contagious.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is benign viral conjunctivitis.

red_flags.description: Sight-threatening red eye — genuinely reduced vision (not just tear-blur), severe deep/aching eye pain, marked photophobia, halos around lights, ciliary flush, or a contact-lens wearer with a red painful eye
red_flags.clinical_significance: Keratitis, uveitis or acute glaucoma can permanently damage sight and need urgent ophthalmology.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Antibiotics don't treat viral conjunctivitis; reserve for clearly bacterial cases.
Not distinguishing benign conjunctivitis from keratitis/uveitis/glaucoma by checking vision, pain and photophobia (and contact-lens use).

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Supportive care + strict contagion control; urgent eye care for any red-eye danger sign (especially contact-lens wearers).

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Viral conjunctivitis is self-limiting; manage with lubricants, cool compresses and strict hygiene/contagion control. Antibiotics are not indicated for viral (or most bacterial) conjunctivitis. Refer red eye with reduced vision, pain or photophobia, and any red painful eye in a contact-lens wearer.
